EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary followed by a large area of low pressure sitting just off the coast facilitated heavy snow over the Cascades from Oct 31st to Nov 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Swift Creek SNOTEL at 4400 ft recorded 14 inches of snow between 0600 PST Oct 30th and 0000 PST Nov 1st. Several other SNOTELS in the vicinity recorded 12+ inches of snow in a similar time period as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low dropped southward into the Pacific Northwest steering a strong frontal boundary and an associated low lever coastal jet into the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet 82SW1 at Cape Disappointment measured a peak gust of 70 mph at 11/13 3-4 am PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary arriving on Nov 17th followed by a modified arctic airmass loft and long-fetch northwesterly flow by Nov 18th facilitate heavy snow across the Cascades above 2000-3000ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: June Lake SNOTEL at 3440 ft recorded 18 inches of snowfall with 12 inches first hit by 0700 Nov 18th and Swift Creek SNOTEL at 4440 ft recorded 15 inches of snow with 12 inches hit around 0100 Nov 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure disturbance embedded within southwest flow aloft brought strong winds to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 82sw1 Cape Disappointment: Peak gust 69 mph 11/22 1300 PST, near or above 58 mph 1100 to 1700. ODT 76 Megler Bridge; peak gust 62 mph 11/22 1127 PST.

CZ_NAME: LOWER SLOPES OF THE EST WASHINGTON CASCADE CREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough followed by amplified W/NW flow aloft brought heavy snow to the Cascades, eastern foothills, and the eastern mountains between the 16th and 19th of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotter 2 miles SE of Cle Elum reported 20 inches of 24-hour snow accumulation around 12:51 PM on the 18th. A weather spotter in Trout Lake reported 8.5 inches of 24-hour snow accumulation at 7:00 AM on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For January 17 through 19, a Low brought another round of widespread snow to Eastern Washington with amounts ranging from 3 to 7 inches across the Basin and Northern Mountains. Cascades had 8 to 12 inches. It was followed by a period of freezing rain on the January 20 and 21. Freezing rain amounts ranged from trace to 2 tenths of inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 6.4 inches of snow near Republic. Other reports in the area ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic front moved into Western Washington. After producing some locally heavy snowfall on the Olympic Peninsula, it was followed by strong Fraser River outflow winds and several days of cold temperatures when a number of record low temperature records were set. It also resulted in several cold weather related deaths in King County. As precipitation moved into the region at the end of the cold episode, heavy snow was reported across the North Interior and freezing rain/drizzle resulted in travel impacts around Puget Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record low temperature of 15 degrees was recorded at Olympia airport. This tied the previous record set in 2007.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low passed through the Pacific Northwest over January 6 through 10. It brought several rounds of locally heavy snow and gusty winds. Through the period, locations in the Cascades and Northern Mountains saw snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches. The Columbia Basin locations had amounts of 1 to 5 inches. Wind gusts ranged from 40 to low 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spokane International Airport gusted to 54 MPH several times on January 9. There were several power outages in the Spokane area with around 150 customers affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening area of low pressure passing over the offshore waters of Western Washington produce very strong easterly winds across portions of Western Washington. The unusual direction of the wind combined with the strength produced extensive tree damage in the Puget Sound region that resulted in damaged homes and vehicles, widespread prolonged power outages, and 2 fatalities from fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Port Townsend recorded a wind gust of 61 mph.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN STRAIT OF JUAN DE FUCA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic front moved into Western Washington. After producing some locally heavy snowfall on the Olympic Peninsula, it was followed by strong Fraser River outflow winds and several days of cold temperatures when a number of record low temperature records were set. It also resulted in several cold weather related deaths in King County. As precipitation moved into the region at the end of the cold episode, heavy snow was reported across the North Interior and freezing rain/drizzle resulted in travel impacts around Puget Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snowfall was reported 2 miles east southeast of Sequim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts. Strong east winds in the Vancouver metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and  fallen power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two feet of snow fell.

The focus of the heavy rain was to the south Washington and north Oregon coast and coastal mountains where 30 hour rain totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches. In addition to the rain, high winds developed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 67 mph at Huckleberry Ridge RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low over British Columbia pushed southward into the Pacific Northwest. This brought low snow levels to much of southwest Washington and northwest Oregon, and the Columbia River Gorge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 64 mph at Cape Disappointment (82SW1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic front moved across Western Washington and Oregon on the 12th and 13th dropping temperatures below freezing. With cold air in the lowlands, all precipitation fell as freezing rain for areas along the coast, valleys in the Coast Range, and Willamette Valley northward into southwest Washington. This caused significant impacts. Strong east winds in the Vancouver metro area resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. There were injuries during this event due to cold exposure, falling trees and  fallen power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions severely impacted travel WA SR-14 through the Gorge. East winds gusted as high as 75 mph at the west end of the Columbia River Gorge. The winds resulted in downed trees/powerlines and power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 60 mph at Huckleberry RAWS (HKFW1) and peak gust of 58 mph at |Longview (AP476).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic front moved into Western Washington. After producing some locally heavy snowfall on the Olympic Peninsula, it was followed by strong Fraser River outflow winds and several days of cold temperatures when a number of record low temperature records were set. It also resulted in several cold weather related deaths in King County. As precipitation moved into the region at the end of the cold episode, heavy snow was reported across the North Interior and freezing rain/drizzle resulted in travel impacts around Puget Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and a half inches of snowfall was recorded 1 mile west southwest of Dungeness. Four inches of snowfall was reported at Port Angeles. Six inches of snowfall was reported 2 miles east southeast of Sequim. Five and a half inches of snowfall was record 2 miles of Agnew.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic front moved into Western Washington. After producing some locally heavy snowfall on the Olympic Peninsula, it was followed by strong Fraser River outflow winds and several days of cold temperatures when a number of record low temperature records were set. As precipitation moved into the region at the end of the cold episode, heavy snow was reported across the North Interior and freezing rain/drizzle resulted in travel impacts around Puget Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tenth of an inch of freezing rain reported 5 miles east northeast of Cedar Falls. Two tenths of an inch of freezing rain reported 1 mile south southwest of Sahalee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system moving in Western Washington encountered a cold air mass already in place. This created an overrunning event that led to wintry precipitation across interior portions of the area from Whatcom County southward into King County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4.2 inches reported at Glacier. Observer near Ferndale reported snowfall total of 4.7 inches. Observer near Blaine reported snowfall total of 4.5 inches. Member of the public reported 4 inches of snowfall at Ferndale. Observer reported 7 inches of snowfall at Newhalem. Member of the public reported 9 inches of snowfall at Blaine. Member of public reported 8 and a half inches of snowfall 5 miles east of Birch Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and cold air advection the night of October 23rd through the morning of the 24th resulted in temperatures cooling below freezing in the Yakima valley in Washington and across north central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Yakima Airport and the AWOS at the Sunnyside Municipal Airport recorded below freezing temperatures for 7 to 10 hours the morning of Oct. 18th. Nearly all stations in the Yakima valley recorded temperatures at or below freezing the morning of Oct. 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass moving in, combined with clear and stable conditions, allowed for widespread freezing temperatures to occur across the forecast area the morning of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op stations D6278 and K7DTF reported subfreezing temperatures between 1am and 8am the morning of the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure persisted over the PacNW for nearly a week. The upper ridge resulted in hot temperatures developing across the region, with widespread moderate to major HeatRisk. Several counties across the region opened cooling centers for the general population.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Franklin county coroner confirmed that a 61 year old male east of Pasco, WA had died of hyperthermia, with alcohol intoxication a contributing factor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For January 17 through 19, a Low brought another round of widespread snow to Eastern Washington with amounts ranging from 3 to 7 inches across the Basin and Northern Mountains. Cascades had 8 to 12 inches. It was followed by a period of freezing rain on the January 20 and 21. Freezing rain amounts ranged from trace to 2 tenths of inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 10 inches snow 2 miles South of Anatone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough continued across central Oregon into central and eastern Washington, resulting in convective activity from the previous day moving north into this area. While most storms produced weak gusty outflows, a storm in the Goldendale area produced strong winds that resulted in property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public sent videos of strong outflow winds impacting their home, with photos showing damage to a sturdy shed detached from the house. They also reported that their neighbors large trees had been uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 22 a wildfire broke out near White Swan, Washington. The fire destroyed 13 to 17 structures, mostly residential ones, and led to Level 3 evacuations in the area. Critical fire weather conditions, however, were not observed in the area around the fire, as winds were light at around 13 mph and RHs were in the low 20% range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 22 a wildfire broke out near White Swan, Washington. The fire destroyed 13 to 17 structures, mostly residential ones, and led to Level 3 evacuations in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shower produced a funnel cloud near La Center, Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video posted by the general public on Facebook showed rotation within a funnel cloud. The video was taken from La Center, Washington and showed the funnel to the northwest. The location is an estimate from the video and radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level low with cold front created high winds from the Long Beach Peninsula of Washington southward to the central Oregon Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Megler Bridge had a 61 mph gust, winds likely were strong over entire Columbia river fetch into Washington with a 48 mph at Ocean Park from 10/25 2226-2231 PST, Toke Point had 52 mph at 2312 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary followed by a large area of low pressure sitting just off the coast facilitated heavy snow over the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Swift Creek SNOTEL at 4400 ft recorded 14 inches of snow between 0600 PST Oct 30th and 1100 PST Oct 31st. Several other SNOTELS in the vicinity recorded 12+ inches of snow in a similar time period as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pioneer Fire started on June 8 by unknown human cause. It was finally contained on October 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pioneer Fire continued through the month of September. It started on June 8. It is located 10 miles Southeast of Stehekin, Washington. It burned 38735 acres of timber and short grass while active. The cost of the fire is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Goosmus Fire was started on September 25. A downed powerline was the cause. While there are no wind reports, lightning tracker showed thunderstorms in the region. It was contained on October 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Goosmus Fire was started on September 25 by a downed powerline. It continued through October 4. It burned 1738 acres of mixed timber, brush, and short grass. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Jack Wells Fire started on October 1 by unknown causes. It is located 3 miles Northwest of Bridgeport. It was contained on October 3. It burned 5335 acres of grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jack Wells Fire started on October 1 by unknown causes. It is located 3 miles Northwest of Bridgeport. It was contained on October 3. It burned 5335 acres of grass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave embedded within a broader upper-level trough increased shower and thunderstorm activity across the region on the afternoon of Oct. 15th. Enough shear was available to produce rotation in a cell near Battle Ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showing what looks like an actual funnel cloud. Weak rotation evident on radar with a shower during the time of the report. No debris was being lofted, so confidence is high this was not a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms impacted the region after a frontal passage with cold air advecting overhead encouraging instability. Enough shear was present to facilitate a waterspout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived waterspout over the Columbia River near the Lewis and Clark bridge at Longview occurred with multiple photos taken from the Lewis and Clark Bridge to the west. Indicated on radar and media reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow aloft followed by high pressure allowed for freezing temperatures to settle in over the lowest elevations of the forecast area the night of October 29 into the morning of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Pasco and Richland ASOSs reported sub-freezing temps between 3am and 8am the morning of the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow aloft followed by high pressure allowed for freezing temperatures to settle in over the lowest elevations of the forecast area the night of October 29 into the morning of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op stations based around The Dalles area reported sub-freezing temperatures between 6am and 830am the morning of the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region bringing strong winds to Eastern Washington. It brought widespread sustained winds in the 20s and gusts into the upper 40 to upper 50 MPH range. Strongest winds were over Southeast Washington. No major impacts were reported. Patchy blowing dusts and small limbs down were the main impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station at Alder measured 57 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong south-southwesterly upper-level low triggered a round of severe thunderstorms and strong outflow winds along the east slopes of the Cascades, Reports of severe hail were received from the public.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Washington fire weather employee and a member of the public on Facebook both reported hail size of around 1 inch near the town of Trout Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong south-southwesterly upper-level low triggered a round of severe thunderstorms and strong outflow winds along the east slopes of the Cascades, Reports of severe hail were received from the public.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations along the Columbia River in Klickitat County recorded outflow winds as high as 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave allowed for strong winds to mix down to the surface of the Columbia Basin during the afternoon and evening hours of November 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op station AU155 in Prosser recorded a 73 mph gust on the evening of the 4th, and a Hanford Site station (HEOC) recorded a gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers resulted in an observed funnel cloud close to the Columbia River in Vancouver, WA. The funnel cloud was observed from Portland, Oregon as well as from Vancouver, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived funnel cloud was observed close to the Columbia River in Vancouver, WA by multiple people in Vancouver, Washington and from across the river in Portland Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough destabilized atmosphere environment that led to numerous thunderstorms and heavy rain. Several thunderstorms occurred over the crest of the Cascades near Easy and Rainy Passes that caused multiple flash floods and debris flows off the mountain ridge along and northeast of State Route 20 that buried the highway in multiple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a couple of thunderstorms fell over the mountain ridge, above the recent Easy Fire burn scar, with the main period of heavy rain lasting about one hour and 45 minutes starting 1800PST. Flash floods and debris flows came out of several drainages along and northeast of State Route 20 between mile post 151 and 152.5, burying the highway. The debris flows initiated above the burn scar and little of the flood debris was from the burn scar itself. It took two weeks to clean up and fix the highway to make it passable. |Rain rates from a nearby rain gage along the highway were up to 0.29 inches in 15 minutes, 0.53 inches in 30 minutes, 0.96 inches in 1 hour, and a two hour storm total of 1.28 inches. No injuries or fatalities occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal boundary followed by an unsettled period of lower snow-level and the arrival of a potent secondary low pressure system brought heavy snow to the Cascades and high winds to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The June Lake SNOTEL at 3440 ft recorded 15 inches of snowfall between 0600 12/25 and 0100 12/26 with 12 inches of snow hit at 2200 12/25, the Spencer Meadow SNOTEL at 3400 ft recorded 15 inches of snowfall between 0800 12/25 and 0000 12/26 with 12 inches of snow hit at 2200 12/25, and finally the Lone Pine SNOTEL at 3940 ft recorded 19 inches of snow between 1300 12/25 and 1100 12/26 with 12 inches hit at 2300 12/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal boundary followed by an unsettled period of lower snow-level and the arrival of a potent secondary low pressure system brought heavy snow to the Cascades and high winds to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station 82SW1 Cape Disappointment measured a peak gust of 68 mph on 12/26 0200 PST and was near or above 58 mph 0100 12/26 to 0600 12/26. ODT76 Megler Bridge measured a peak gust of 66 mph 12/26 0326 PST and was near or above 58mph 12/25 2300 to 12/26 0500.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal boundary followed by an unsettled period of lower snow-level and the arrival of a potent secondary low pressure system brought heavy snow to the Cascades and high winds to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Abernathy Mtn RAWS measured a peak gust of 64 mph at 12/26 0800 PST and was near or above 58 mph 12/26 0000 to 12/26 0900.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a warm frontal boundary and mature surface low facilitated high winds along the coast into the coast range. This aforementioned frontal boundary also brought a rise in snow levels increasing river levels, especially those following out of the Willapa Hills where localized river flooding was present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station 82sw1 Cape Disappointment measured a peak gust of 67 mph on 12/18 0200 PST and was near or above 58 mph 2100 12/17 to 0300 12/18. Also, the mesonet station ODT76 Megler Bridge measured a peak gust of 80 mph on 12/18 0106 PST and was near or above 58mph 12/17 1400 to 12/18 0330.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving, heavy rain showers moved over the the higher terrain near Wenatchee. Rain amounts ranged from 0.4 to 0.6 of an inch from 1700-2000 PST. The runoff was funneled through the canyons and caused flash flooding for parts of Western Wenatchee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chelan County dispatch office reported water flowing out of manhole covers and flooding streets, including at the corner of Maple and Northwestern Avenue. Report on Facebook stated water was up to bumpers of vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high astronomical tides, large surf, and rainfall resulted in flooding of highways and roads in low-lying areas near Willapa Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several highways and roads in the low-lying areas around Willapa Bay flooded due to the combination of high astronomical tides, large surf, and rainfall. A surge of 2.4 ft MLLW was calculated by subtracting the astronomical tide height from the observed total water level from the NOAA NOS Toke Point Tide observing station. Highway 101 flooded in three locations resulting in closures that impacted the city of Raymond. The South Bend Highway was closed west of Naselle near the State Route 4 intersection. Water and rocks across State Route 105 impacted travel near mile marker 20. Tokeland Penn near Toke Point had 3 ft water across it with 12 to 18 inches of water observed across Lower Peter Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged stratus under an upper level ridge pattern brought periods of freezing fog and light freezing drizzle across the region. Locations across the region reported amounts of trace to 0.1 of ice accumulation. It caused hazardous travel conditions leading to vehicle accidents across the region. Two fatal wrecks were attributed to the icy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0815 PST, a tanker containing fuel crashed and caught fire 12 miles west of Cheney near milepost 266. One fatality was reported. Interstate 90 was closed for 2 hours. Light freezing rain was occuring at the time with Spokane Airport ASOS reporting trace amounts. It is believed to be have been a contributing factor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1545 PST, a vehicle containing 9 people crashed at the SR-395 interchange along I-90 near Ritzville. One fatality and 9 injuries were reported. EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 0625 PST, SPD responded to a commercial vehicle versus tree collision at Highway 2 near Sunset Frontage Road.  Initial callers reported that a tree had fallen onto the highway and struck an eastbound truck, causing the truck to leave the roadway.  Spokane Police and Washington State Patrol responded and found that the two occupants of the truck were deceased at the scene.  Collision investigators with the SPD Major Crimes Unit (MCU) also responded to assist with the investigation.  Highway 2 (eastbound) was briefly shutdown during the investigation. At this time, investigators believe that heavy winds this morning caused the tree to dislodge from the ground and fall across the roadway. Spokane Airport ASOS recorded a gusts of 30 MPH at the time of incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry cold front pushed through the region on September 25. It generated several thunderstorms over Eastern Washington as it passed. It severe gusty outflow winds from downdrafts. The winds picked up the dry, loose dirt causing dust storm conditions and also damages to trees and roofs. Recorded wind gusts ranged from 50 to 69 MPH. There several reports of power outages, 2437 in Pend Oreille, 12394 in Spokane, 7471 in Stevens, 790 in Whitman counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branch broke off of tree and crushed the top of a vehicle. Pullman, Moscow Airport recorded a gust of 67 MPH.

There several reports of power outages, 2437 in Pend Oreille, 12394 in Spokane, 7471 in Stevens, 790 in Whitman counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed power started a fire. Spokane International Airport recorded a gust of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low dropped southward into the Pacific Northwest steering a strong frontal boundary and an associated low lever coastal jet into the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HKFW1 Huckleberry Ridge RAWS: Peak gust of 60 mph 11/12 2111 PST, was gusting near or above 58 mph from 2000 11/12 to 0200 11/13. Nearby Abernathy Mtn RAWS hit a peak gust of 57 mph, 1 mph under criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally strong low pressure system over the eastern Pacific and an accompanying frontal boundary brought locally strong winds to the Willapa Hills November 19th into the early portion of November 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HKFW1 Huckleberry Ridge RAWS: Peak gust of 65 mph 11/19 1711, was gusting near criteria from 1500 11/19 to 2200 11/19. Nearby Abernathy Mtn RAWS hit a peak gust of 57 mph, 1 mph under criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong band of SW flow aloft associated with a deep offshore low allowed for very strong wind gusts to occur across the foothills of the Blue Mountains during the overnight period of November 19-20. This system also produced heavy snow and blizzard like conditions along portions of the east slopes of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-Op station AS070 recorded a 70 mph gust, and the Walla Walla ASOS recorded a gust of 61 mph overnight the 19th into the 20th of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Goosmus Fire was started on September 25. A downed powerline was the cause. While there are no wind reports, lightning tracker showed thunderstorms in the region. It was contained on October 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Goosmus Fire was started on September 25 by a downed powerline. It continued through October 4. It burned 1738 acres of mixed timber, brush, and short grass. Cost is estimated.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SLOPES OF THE EST WASHINGTON CASCADE CREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong band of SW flow aloft associated with a deep offshore low allowed for very strong wind gusts to occur across the foothills of the Blue Mountains during the overnight period of November 19-20. This system also produced heavy snow and blizzard like conditions along portions of the east slopes of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington Department of Transportation reported blizzard like conditions via social media for over 3 hours along Interstate 90 in Snoqualmie pass.  A trained spotter 25 miles southwest of South Cle Elum reported 14 inches of new snow by 0700 PST on November 20th. The White Pass SNOTEL reported 12 inches of new snow in a 14 hour period ending 0400 on November 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low brought an early season snow across the northern mountains of Eastern Washington. It brought snow amounts ranging from 7 to 12 inches to elevations above 3500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington DOT reported an estimated 12 inches of snow had fallen at Sherman Pass along state route 20 and still snowing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prior snow and rain the day before led to icy road conditions on November 17. It caused hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi-truck lost control on the icy road and jackknifed into the opposing lane around 0720 AM PST. This cascaded into a multi-vehicle accident involving 3 cars and 2 semi-trucks total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern Washington had several days of near freezing temperatures. A strong low pressure with a decent plume of moisture during the November 19 through November 21 time period brought the first widespread snow event of the winter. The Cascades received the bulk of the snow with ranges of 6 to 16 inches. The rest of the region had up to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 13.8 inches of snow was measured 1 mile North of Winton. Other reports in area ranged from 4 to 9 inches for locations Leavenworth, Plain, and Dryden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front pushed through the region on September 29. It brought strong winds ranging from 40 to 53 MPH across the Columbia Basin. The region had very little precipitation for several weeks prior and also saw an increase in agricultural activity for the season. The winds picked up the dry, loose dirt and caused a dust storm stretching from Soap Lake area into the Pullman area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported dust storm reduced visibility to less than two car lengths near Soap Lake. A public report stated a dust channel was moving west to east 10 miles North of Moses Lake and reduced visibility to less than half a mile. Time is estimated from satellite data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of dust storm reducing visibility to around quarter of mile during the afternoon. The area covered was Oakesdale to South of Palouse and along state route 195 near Steptoe to the the Idaho border. Time is estimated from satellite data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dust storm reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile. Trained spotter reported unable to see roadway. A two car wreck along Highway 21 was attributed to incident. The area coverage of dust was between Highway 21, I-90, and state route 28. Locations included Odessa, Mohler, and Ritzville. The stretch of I-90 through Ritzville is heavily traveled and many reports centered here. Time is estimated by satellite data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A marginally unstable atmosphere and breezy winds in the region resulted in a landspout developing in far northeastern Kittitas county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video taken from George, WA showed a landspout travelling across far northeastern Kittitas county. The event time is estimated based on radar trends and time video was received.
